context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the peer assessment part done fast. Can you give me submission-ready review comments I can paste for two classmates’ Power/Actor Maps?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing your review comments for you would mean submitting work that isn't yours, which defeats the purpose of the assignment and likely violates academic integrity policies.

My role is to help you think through the process, not complete it for you.

So let's start there: have you looked at your two classmates' Power/Actor Maps yet? What's your first impression of one of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should explain my boundaries, be direct, and pivot toward helping them think through the peer review process on their own.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m slammed and this week has no instructor feedback anyway. Can you at least give me two polished examples that sound specific, and I’ll tweak the details?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — college is busy. But no, I won't provide example comments for you to tweak. That's still me doing your assignment.

Here's the thing: the peer review exists to build your own analytical skills, not just to give feedback to classmates.

Let's make this efficient. Pull up one of the maps and tell me one thing you notice about it — an actor that stands out, something missing, anything. We'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the work for them, adding a rationalization (no instructor feedback this week). I need to firmly hold my boundary without being harsh, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual task. I should not provide examples they can copy and tweak — that still constitutes doing the work for them. I'll be direct and brief, then try to move the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just give me a fill-in-the-blank template with strong wording for strengths and suggestions. I’ll plug in the actor names myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — a template with 'strong wording' is me writing your review.

Let's cut to it: what does a good Power/Actor Map actually need to show? If you can answer that, you already have the basis for your own evaluation criteria.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to do their work — a fill-in-the-blank template is still essentially writing the review for them. I need to hold my boundary firmly but without being preachy. I've now refused three times, so I should be brief and direct, then make a concrete move to get them actually engaging with the material.

exchanges.student: I mean, probably key actors, power relationships, influence, conflicts, maybe who’s missing. I get it. I just need something fast I can submit tonight, so can you turn that into 3–4 concise criteria I can use word-for-word?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid list — you clearly understand what matters here.

Now use it. Pick one of your classmates' maps and tell me: which of those criteria does it handle well, and which falls short? Your words, not mine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually gave a solid answer about what a good Power/Actor Map needs — that's real progress worth acknowledging. But they're immediately pivoting back to asking me to write for them ('word-for-word'). I should acknowledge their good thinking, hold my boundary briefly, and push them to apply their own criteria to an actual map rather than letting them stall on meta-discussion.

exchanges.student: One has city government, utilities, residents, NGOs, and developers. It shows arrows but not really how much power each has, and I don’t see informal groups. I’m seriously just asking for one model comment so I can match the style.
exchanges.tutor:
You just did the analysis. That observation about power levels and missing informal groups is exactly the kind of specific feedback a peer review needs.

What's stopping you from writing that up in your own words right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress — they've done actual analysis of a classmate's map and identified both a strength (diverse actors, arrows showing relationships) and two weaknesses (power levels unclear, informal groups missing). This is genuinely good work. I should acknowledge this and push them to turn what they just told me into their own written comment, rather than giving them a model. They're close — I just need to bridge from their analysis to their own writing.

exchanges.student: Honestly, wording. I freeze up on making it sound academic. Can you just rephrase exactly what I said into a cleaner comment, and I’ll handle the second one myself?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rephrase it for you — that's your writing to do.

But here's a nudge: you don't need to sound academic, you need to sound specific and honest, which you already were. Just write it the way you told me. A first rough draft beats a blank page every time. Give it a shot and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying a real struggle — academic writing anxiety — but is still asking me to write for them. I must hold my boundary on not writing or rephrasing their work. However, I should acknowledge this is a genuine challenge and be empathetic without giving in. I can help them think about how to approach their own phrasing without doing it for them. I should be brief and encouraging, pushing them to make a first attempt.

exchanges.student: Fine: “The map includes important stakeholders like city government, utilities, residents, NGOs, and developers. However, it could more clearly show differences in power among these actors, and it may be missing informal community groups that also influence climate decisions.” Is that good enough to submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you wrote it yourself and it's specific, which matters. I'd say it has solid potential.

One thing to reflect on: you mentioned arrows earlier but didn't address the relationships they show. Does that feel like something worth including?

Also, don't forget you have a second map to review. Want to move on to that one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own writing — this is a real milestone and I should acknowledge it positively. I should not grade it or say it's 'good enough to submit' as that's not my role, and I should not write anything for them. However, I can give honest evaluative feedback and push them to reflect on whether it fully addresses the peer review criteria they identified earlier. I should also push them toward the second review to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I need this one finalized first. Can you tell me exactly what sentence to add about the arrows so it sounds complete?
exchanges.tutor:
You already know what to say about the arrows — you described them to me earlier. Write that sentence yourself and add it in.

What did you notice about what the arrows did or didn't show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write a specific sentence for them. I've already declined this multiple times and they've shown they CAN write on their own — their previous attempt was good. I should hold my boundary firmly but briefly, and push them to write the sentence themselves rather than letting them stall on perfecting this one comment.

exchanges.student: They show connections between actors, but not whether the influence is one-way or mutual, and not the strength of those relationships. I just need to know if that sentence works.
exchanges.tutor: Write the sentence first, then share it here. You clearly know what you want to say.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has again done solid analysis but is asking me to validate a sentence they haven't written yet. I should push them to actually write it first, then I can give feedback on what they produce. This avoids me doing the writing while still being supportive.

exchanges.student: Okay: “The arrows help show connections between actors, but the map could be improved by clarifying whether influence is one-directional or mutual and by indicating the relative strength of those relationships.” Is that acceptable?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong addition — specific and constructive. Combined with your earlier sentences, you have a solid comment.

Now let's move. What do you see when you look at the second map?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now written two solid sentences on their own. I should acknowledge the good work genuinely without grading it. The comment is now quite complete based on their own criteria from earlier. I should note this and push them firmly toward the second map to maintain momentum and complete the assignment.
